exemplar.title: At which stage of the Butlerâs tourism development model is Intramuros, Philippines, located and what role has perceptual carrying capacity played in determining the stage?

detailedExemplarData.title: At which stage of the Butlerâs tourism development model is Intramuros, Philippines, located and what role has perceptual carrying capacity played in determining the stage?

subject.title: Geography
subject.description:
**IB Geography** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **physical and human geography, environmental interactions, global challenges, and spatial patterns**, with a strong focus on **fieldwork, data analysis, case studies, and Geographic Information Systems (GIS)**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Geographic themes - structured questions | 2h 15min | 2h 15min | SL: 35% / HL: 35% |
| **Paper 2** | Geographic perspectives - data response and essays | 1h 15min | 1h 15min | SL: 40% / HL: 45% |
| **Paper 3** | Global interactions - extended essay responses | N/A | 1h | HL: 20% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Fieldwork investigation (2,500 words) | 20 hours | 20 hours | SL: 25% / HL: 20% |

strengths: Research question is clearly geographical with explicit theoretical grounding allowing hypothesis formation
Precise focus on Intramuros with demonstrated feasibility for primary data collection
Three wellâconstructed maps follow conventions and effectively situate the study area
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands of Criterion A meet the topâband descriptors: the question is explicitly linked to Butlerâs lifeâcycle theory and perceptual carrying capacity, the location is precisely defined and feasible for onâsite data collection, and mapping conventions are fully adhered to. Thus, the full 3 marks are justified.
feedback.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

strengths: Methods (questionnaire, pedestrian counts) are clearly described and linked to the research question and theoretical framework
Dataâcollection techniques are correctly implemented, yielding usable primary data
weaknesses: Rationale for methodological choices (e.g., threeâminute count interval, kâmeans cluster k=2) is not provided
Convenience sampling and singleâday fieldwork limit reliability and introduce bias
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The methods are described with clear relevance (band 2) and techniques are correctly used (band 2), but the lack of deeper theoretical justification and limited sampling prevent a bandâ3 award. Therefore, 2 marks accurately reflect performance.

strengths: A wide variety of appropriate presentation techniques (tables, charts, plots) enhance interpretation
Most data are directly relevant to the investigation
Axes and sources are generally labelled correctly
weaknesses: Occasional omissions in conventions (missing units, truncated legends)
Nonâresponse and sample biases reduce data sufficiency
Some tables and figures use ambiguous headings that hinder clarity
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The information quality sits solidly in the 3-4 band: presentation is effective overall, data are relevant but have sufficiency limitations, and conventions are followed with occasional errors. A score of 4 best captures this level of performance.
feedback.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

strengths: Uses appropriate descriptive and statistical techniques (growth rates, t-tests, Mann-Whitney) correctly
Interprets trends and anomalies with links to Butlerâs model and perceptual carrying capacity
Analysis covers all hypotheses with systematic explanations
weaknesses: Lacks confidence intervals and effectâsize reporting to strengthen statistical rigour
No regression or more advanced inferential techniques to reach the top band
Some theoretical metrics (e.g., for perceptual capacity) remain underdeveloped
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The written analysis demonstrates competent use of statistical tests and clear interpretation (band 5), with some gaps in advanced techniques and completeness that prevent a band-6 or higher. Awarding 5 marks appropriately reflects the depth and limitations of the analysis.

strengths: Conclusion directly answers the research question and is fully supported by the analysis
No new material is introduced, demonstrating coherence with preceding sections
weaknesses: Could be strengthened by discussing practical management implications for stakeholders
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion meets the top descriptor for Criterion E by providing a clear, fully supported answer to both parts of the question. Hence, the maximum 2 marks are justified.

strengths: Appropriate strengths and weaknesses of the investigation are identified
Relevant improvement suggestions (e.g., multiâday sampling, alternative sampling frames) are outlined
weaknesses: Explanations of how suggested improvements would impact validity are brief
Limited discussion of methodological alternatives beyond basic suggestions
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation outlines key strengths and weaknesses and provides relevant suggestions, aligning with band 2. However, it lacks the comprehensive depth and impact analysis required for band 3, so 2 marks accurately represent its quality.

strandSelections.reasoning: Three well-constructed maps (country, metropolitan, and detailed site map) follow cartographic conventions: scale bars, north arrows, legible annotation and a legend.  The maps are uncluttered, directly support the investigation and clearly locate data-collection points, fulfilling the top-band requirement for âclear locational maps following mapping conventions.â

The research question is explicitly geographical, rooted in Butlerâs life-cycle theory and the concept of perceptual carrying capacity.  It is tightly focused on a single destination (Intramuros) and invites testable hypotheses that are later stated.  The theoretical link is clear enough to allow statistical testing of stated expectations, satisfying the âhypothesis formationâ element of the top descriptor.

A wide variety of mostly effective presentation techniques are used: tables, pie charts, box-and-whisker plots, heat-map overlay, line graph and annotated model diagram.  Visuals are generally appropriate and enhance interpretation, fulfilling the higher end of the 3-4 band.

Data-collection techniques are correctly implemented (61 questionnaires, replicated counts, averaging) but remain limited: convenience sampling, single-day fieldwork and evident non-response reduce reliability.  Thus they sit above band 1 (techniques used incorrectly) yet fall short of band 3âs âreliable, high-quality primary data.â

Analysis goes well beyond description: growth-rate calculations, k-means clustering, t-tests and Mann-Whitney tests are correctly applied and interpreted.  Confidence intervals are absent, and the statistical depth, while strong, is not exhaustive (no regression or advanced inferential work), placing it solidly at 5 within the 5-6 band.

Trends, anomalies and their significance for Butlerâs model are systematically explained; primary perceptions are linked back to perceptual carrying capacity and theory.  Some links (e.g., seasonal Holy Week spike) could be deeper, so the work earns 5 rather than 6.

The student specifies Intramuros, a walled district inside Metro Manila, and justifies its tourism relevance.  Primary data collection on site is demonstrably feasible (questionnaire and pedestrian counts conducted there).  Detailed background (population, maps) shows an excellent grasp of geographic context and meets the requirement for a âprecise location with clear feasibility for primary data collection.â

The evaluation identifies key strengths and weaknesses (sample size, weekend bias, positive-visitor bias).  Discussion is relevant but not deeply elaborated (e.g., no reflection on methodological alternatives beyond brief mention), placing it at the appropriate outline level of band 2.

All data presented (survey results, counts, secondary visitor statistics) are directly relevant to assessing Butler stage and perceptual capacity.  Sample size and single-day focus limit sufficiency, so the strand fits the lower half of the 3-4 band (âmost data relevant allowing partial analysisâ).

The conclusion clearly answers both parts of the research question and is explicitly based on the analytical findings, with no new material introduced.  This matches the descriptor for a fully supported conclusion (band 2).

The analysis covers all data sets and hypotheses, but acknowledged limitations (single visit, small sample) imply residual gaps.  Thus it meets âanalysis with some gaps in evidence,â meriting 5 in the 5-6 range.

Methods (questionnaire, pedestrian counts) are described and explicitly linked to the Butler model and perceptual carrying capacity.  However, explanation is brief (â250 words) and lacks full theoretical alignmentâfor example, no rationale for the 3-minute count period or cluster analysis choice is provided.  This matches the mid-band description of âmethods are described with clear relevance,â rather than the comprehensive detail expected in band 3.

Titles and sources are given and axes are usually labelled, but a few figures have minor omissions (e.g., units on box plot, some legends truncated).  These âoccasional errors in conventionsâ align with the middle level of the 3-4 band.

Improvement suggestionsâlarger multi-day sample, alternative sampling to include deterred tourists, even-number Likert scalesâare relevant and include brief comments on how they would enhance validity.  While helpful, the explanations lack the detailed impact analysis required for the top band, so band 2 is most fitting.

annotations.comment: Justify the choice of a threeâminute interval for pedestrian counts by discussing its tradeâoff between practicality and data reliability.
Table 1 provides frequencies by nationality; adding percentage columns would enhance clarity and comparative analysis.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Nonâresponse in Table 4 (frequency of travel) reduces data sufficiency; discuss its impact on analysis reliability.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: The research question is succinctly formulated with clear theoretical grounding in Butlerâs model and perceptual carrying capacity, allowing for testable hypotheses.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The introduction cites a general definition of tourism but should deepen the theoretical context by discussing multiple dimensions of carrying capacity beyond the single source.
The inclusion of clear, wellâlabelled locational maps at multiple scales effectively situates Intramuros within its national and urban contexts.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The first hypothesis would benefit from citing empirical studies linking rising incomes to tourism competition to strengthen its theoretical justification.
Hypothesis 2 mentions traffic and parking but lacks a theoretical metric for perceptual carrying capacity; clarify how these factors quantitatively impact visitor perceptions.
The convenience sampling approach is described but alternatives (e.g., stratified sampling) are not considered; discuss how these could reduce bias.
The annotated questionnaire figure shows inconsistent orientation of Likert scales; unify higherâvalue interpretation across questions to avoid respondent confusion.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: The perception table uses overlapping headings (âRating of 1â and â1â); simplify or relabel columns to avoid ambiguity.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: The linear interpolation of COVIDâperiod tourist numbers needs methodological justification; consider more robust imputation techniques.
Format the growth rate formula using display math: $$\mathrm{Growth}_t=\frac{N_t - N_{t-1}}{N_{t-1}}$$ for clarity.
Including confidence intervals around growthârate estimates would enhance statistical rigor and interpretability.
The sample is heavily weighted toward younger visitors (67% under 30); discuss how this skews conclusions and generalizability.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Figure 10 and 11 show distributions but omit variability measures; consider including error bars or summary statistics for clarity.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Conclusions drawn from means near the midpoint require formal hypothesis testing against a neutral threshold to substantiate claims.
The boxâandâwhisker plot lacks axis labels and unit definitions; full adherence to conventions is necessary for clarity.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: The choice of k=2 in the k-means cluster analysis is unexplained; justify this selection using silhouette or elbowâmethod diagnostics.
Cluster tables label groups generically; use descriptive names (e.g., âFrequent Travelersâ vs âLeisure Day-trippersâ) to aid reader comprehension.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Report effect sizes (e.g., Cohenâs d) alongside tâtest p-values to provide insight into the magnitude of observed differences.
The Mann-Whitney results should include the U statistic and a note on distribution assumptions to fully interpret the non-parametric test.
The conclusion is well supported but could be enriched by discussing practical management implications for Intramuros stakeholders.
The suggestion to use even-number Likert scales is insightful; additionally, pilot testing could identify confusing items before full deployment.
Temporal bias is noted (weekend data only); extend pedestrian counts to weekdays and varied times to capture daily visitor patterns.
